images.original_text:
燈八寸
後總同前總數但用黑油紙匾形高一尺二寸
橫二尺區四十隊燈高八寸橫一尺六寸匾四
寸
中總塘報等燈俱圓而黃小止用八寸
一凡各每燈一盞用黑油布四層罩蓋一箇以備
一時遮隱使寸明不露或明營暗從或暗營倏
明為莫測之巧也
四罩蓋圖
兵腰牌陰面
隊長腰樑陰面
哨長腰牌陰面

哨官旗
某营哨長某人
千某哨長某人
百某哨長某人
某營四哨長某人
總入五哨長某人
桿高四尺六分
旗方一尺二寸色
旗

把總旗
某子前哨官某人
左哨官某人
右哨官某人
把總後哨官某人
桿高三尺五分旗
一尺五寸旗色

中軍旗
三軍旗令
桿高三尺徑二旗尺二
寸帶長一至寸隨意色

紀効新書卷一終
主將腰旗
三軍腰旗
得高一尺令壺分
旗守當五色接
圖之二變分左右為焉長隊
陣禽寫

二位各變原右一三才陣之圖
變伍一左原
寫為變才三陣之圖

夜入探海勢
乃持鎗行立看
守之法遇敵變
勢隨機應用無
不中節

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance with left foot slightly advanced, body angled slightly to the right. Right arm extended forward holding a spear horizontally, tip pointing left. Left arm bent at elbow, hand raised near shoulder level in a defensive or preparatory gesture. Weight distributed evenly between both feet, core engaged for balance and readiness.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Night Intrusion Sea-Exploring Posture' — a tactical stance for night combat where one holds a spear ready while observing the enemy. The posture emphasizes adaptability: when encountering an opponent, the technique adapts dynamically to the situation without losing rhythm or precision ('勢隨機應用無不中節').

images.original_text:
四夷賔服勢
乃中平鎗法為六合鎗之主作二十四勢之元妙變無窮自古迄今各械鮮有當其鋒諸勢莫可同其趣

persons.posture: Stance: Wide, low horse stance with knees bent and body slightly crouched. Feet are spread apart, weight evenly distributed. Hand position: Both hands grip a long spear, one near the tip, the other closer to the midshaft. Body angle: Slightly turned sideways, facing left. The spear extends horizontally to the left, held firmly in both hands, ready for thrust or defensive maneuver.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Four Barbarians Subdued Posture' from the 'Middle-Level Spear Method,' which is the foundational technique of the Six Harmonies Spear system. It represents the origin of twenty-four spear forms, each with infinite variations. Historically, no other weapon has matched its sharpness or versatility. The posture demonstrates readiness for both offense and defense, embodying the core principle of balanced martial application.

images.original_text:
指南針勢
乃上平鋒法
其類用近乎
中平而着數
不離六合之
變有心演悟
二十四勢之
中可破其半

persons.posture: Standing in a stable, grounded stance with feet shoulder-width apart, body slightly turned sideways. Left arm extended forward holding the spear shaft horizontally at chest height, right arm bent at elbow gripping the spear near the base. Spear tip points left. Weight distributed evenly between both legs, core engaged for balance and control.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Zhinan Zhen Shi' (指南針勢) technique — a foundational spear posture emphasizing horizontal alignment and readiness to pivot or strike. The text describes it as an upper-level spear method using close-range positioning ('近乎中平'), maintaining the six harmonies ('六合') through internal awareness ('有心演悟'). It is part of a set of twenty-four spear forms ('二十四勢'), capable of breaking half the opponent’s defense ('可破其半').

images.original_text:
十面埋伏勢
乃下平鎗法門
戶緊於上平機
巧不亞中式精
於此者諸勢可
降

persons.posture: Kneeling stance with left knee on ground, right leg bent and foot planted for stability. Body leans forward slightly, weight distributed low and centered. Both hands grip a long spear horizontally, extending forward with the tip pointing left. Head turned slightly to the left, eyes focused ahead. Arms are extended but relaxed, suggesting readiness for thrust or defensive maneuver.
persons.tactic: This is the 'Ten-Faced Ambush Posture' (十面埋伏勢), a tactical formation using the spear in a low, stable guard position. The technique emphasizes control of space and timing, with the spear held horizontally to block or counter incoming attacks while maintaining balance for rapid response. The phrase '戶緊於上平機' suggests the lower body is tightly anchored to support the upper body’s offensive or defensive actions, while '巧不亞中式精' implies this posture is as skillful as standard Chinese martial arts forms.

images.original_text:
青龍獻爪勢
乃仍鴛出羣鋒
法勢之中看
着之內發鋒削
人不離是法

persons.posture: Crouched stance with left leg forward and right leg back, body leaning slightly forward. Right arm extended forward holding a spear pointed diagonally downward; left arm bent back for balance. Weight distributed mostly on front leg, head turned toward the spear tip, eyes focused ahead. The posture suggests a low, aggressive thrust or strike.
persons.tactic: This is the 'Green Dragon Presents Claws' technique — a dynamic spear thrusting maneuver emphasizing sudden, penetrating attack from a low, grounded stance. The movement combines footwork, body alignment, and weapon extension to overwhelm the opponent’s defense by striking through their guard.

images.original_text:
邊攔勢
乃裡把門封閉
鎗法守門戶有
經抵顛擊閃賭
上穿指袖股倚
他出馬一鎗迎
抱着琵琶埋伏

persons.posture: The figure is in a low, wide stance with knees bent and body leaning forward. One leg is extended forward, the other back for balance. The left arm extends forward holding a spear pointed diagonally upward, while the right arm is drawn back near the hip. The head is turned slightly to the side, eyes focused ahead. Weight is distributed mostly on the front leg, suggesting readiness to thrust or parry.
persons.tactic: This depicts the '边拦势' (Side Barrier Posture), a defensive martial technique using a spear to block an incoming attack while preparing to counter. The posture combines guarding the door (门户) with a dynamic thrusting motion, as described in the text: '鎗法守門戶有經抵顛擊閃賭' — using the spear to intercept, deflect, and strike. The phrase '上穿指袖股倚' suggests a penetrating thrust aimed at vital points, while '他出馬一鎗迎' indicates meeting the opponent’s charge with a spear thrust. The final line '抱着琵琶埋伏' implies a hidden or ambush position, possibly indicating the practitioner is lying in wait after the initial engagement.

images.original_text:
鐵翻華勢
乃外把門黃龍點竿鎗法
一截二進蛇
弄風撲着鴛
鴛不放鬆

persons.posture: Crouched stance with left leg forward and right leg back, body angled slightly forward. Left hand grips the spear near the middle, right hand extends toward the tip. Spear held low and extended horizontally to the left. Weight distributed forward on the front leg, torso leaning slightly forward for balance and readiness.
persons.tactic: This depicts a martial application of the 'Iron Flip Flower Style' — specifically the 'Yellow Dragon Pointing Spear Technique'. The movement involves a two-part strike: first a blocking or intercepting motion ('one cut, two advance'), then a sweeping or striking motion resembling a snake's glide ('snake advancing'). The final phrase 'flying wind扑着鸳' suggests a feint or sweeping attack aimed at disorienting or capturing the opponent, while '鸳不放松' implies maintaining control or pressure after the strike.

images.original_text:
騎劍勢
乃裙欄鎗法
大開門戶誘
他來遂我中
途擊刺他虛
我實搖花鎗
他實我虛棚
退敵

persons.posture: Kneeling in a low, wide stance with left knee on ground and right leg bent; body angled forward, weight distributed heavily on front leg; right hand gripping sword hilt near the base, left hand extended along the blade for balance; sword held horizontally at waist level, point directed leftward. Head turned slightly toward the direction of the sword’s tip, eyes focused ahead.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Ride Sword Posture' (騎劍勢), a defensive maneuver using a horizontal sword block to deflect an incoming attack while creating an opening. The practitioner uses the ‘裙欄鎗法’ (skirt-barrier spear method) to lure the opponent into overextending their attack ('大開門戶誘他來遂我中'), then counters by striking their exposed side or center ('途擊刺他虛'). The phrase '我實搖花鎗' suggests a feigned attack or deceptive motion to mislead the opponent, while '他實我虛棚' indicates the opponent is deceived into believing the defender is weak, allowing the real strike to come from an unexpected angle. The final line '退敵' implies this technique is used to repel the enemy.

images.original_text:
鋪地錦勢
乃地蛇鑽法
起手坡挨急
刺高來直擦
難饒若他滴
水認針穿魌
法死中反活

persons.posture: Kneeling on ground, body low and forward-leaning, left leg extended backward for balance, right knee bent, torso twisted slightly to the side. Right hand grips spear shaft near tip, spear pointed diagonally forward and downward. Left arm extended back for counterbalance. Weight distributed low and centered over front foot, suggesting a low-strike or trapping technique.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Ground Snake钻法' (Ground Snake Penetration Technique), where the practitioner uses a low stance to evade or trap an opponent’s leg or weapon while delivering a thrusting spear strike. The phrase '法死中反活' implies turning a seemingly hopeless or defensive position into an offensive advantage — likely by using the spear to pierce through an opponent’s guard while maintaining low center of gravity.

images.original_text:
朝天勢
乃上驚下取鎗
法搖旗掃地鐵
牛耕那怕他拖
刀詭計

persons.posture: The figure is in a low, crouched stance with one knee bent and the other leg extended for balance. The body leans forward slightly, weight distributed primarily on the front leg. The right arm is raised high above the head, gripping a spear vertically with both hands, tip pointing upward. The left arm is drawn back near the hip, aiding in counterbalance. The head is turned slightly to the side, eyes focused forward, suggesting readiness for attack or defense.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the 'Chao Tian Shi' (Heavenward Posture), a martial application where the practitioner uses an upward thrusting spear technique to strike downward from a high position, often used to disrupt an opponent’s balance or to initiate a sweeping attack. The accompanying text suggests combining this with flag-waving motions ('摇旗') to confuse or mislead the enemy, while also implying a deceptive maneuver ('刀诡计') involving feints or sudden changes of direction.

images.original_text:
鐵牛耕地勢
乃急搗雄鑕法硬
去硬回莫軚惟有
此鑕無空他能平
伏閃吾鑕就使黑
龍入洞

persons.posture: Crouched stance with left leg forward and right leg back, body leaning slightly forward. Left hand grips the spear shaft near the tip, right hand positioned lower on the shaft. Spear is extended horizontally to the left, tip pointing outward. Weight distributed mostly on the front (left) leg, back (right) leg providing stability. Head turned toward the direction of the spear’s extension, eyes focused ahead.
persons.tactic: This depicts a grounded, defensive yet aggressive spear technique known as 'Iron Ox Plowing Ground' — emphasizing low center of gravity, rapid thrusting motion, and control over space. The text instructs to strike hard and then retreat quickly, avoiding empty movements; the spear must never be idle. The phrase '伏閃吾鑕就使黑龍入洞' suggests using evasive sidesteps while maintaining spear pressure, likening the movement to a black dragon entering a cave — implying fluidity, concealment, and sudden lethal strike.

images.original_text:
滴水勢
乃提顛之法順手
鳳點頭披撲中取
巧進勢用騎龍出
可棚退勇若還破
低勢難同伏地鉛
百發百中

persons.posture: Stance: Low, forward-leaning with bent knees and weight shifted to front leg. Hand position: Right hand grips spear near tip, left hand supports mid-shaft. Body angle: Torso angled forward, head turned slightly toward direction of attack. Foot position: Front foot planted firmly, back foot trailing for balance and mobility. Weight distribution: Approximately 60% on front leg, 40% on back leg.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Drip Water Stance' (滴水勢), a martial technique emphasizing fluid motion and sudden counterattack. The practitioner uses a spear to strike downward or sideward while maintaining low center of gravity to evade or disrupt opponent’s advance. The text describes using this stance to 'take advantage of the opponent’s momentum' (鳳點頭披撲中取) and 'advance skillfully' (巧進勢用騎龍出), suggesting an offensive maneuver that combines evasion with decisive thrusting.

images.original_text:
騎龍勢
刀拘步鎗法
進有撥單尋
蛇退有逼攔
救護梨花滾
袖似穿梭四
面是鑄雲罩
霧

persons.posture: The figure stands in a wide, grounded stance with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. The left leg is forward, right leg back for balance. The body leans slightly forward with torso angled, weight distributed mostly on the front leg. Both arms are extended horizontally, holding a spear pointed straight ahead. The head is turned to face the direction of the spear tip, eyes focused forward. The right hand grips the spear near the shaft, left hand further back for control and balance.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the 'Riding Dragon Stance' (騎龍勢), a defensive and offensive martial technique using a spear. The movement involves advancing with a sweeping or deflecting motion ('拨单寻') while maintaining readiness to retreat or counter ('蛇退有逼攔'). The description suggests fluid transitions between attack and defense, resembling the flow of plum blossoms ('梨花滾') and weaving like a shuttle ('穿梭四'), indicating rapid, multi-directional movements. The phrase '面是鑄雲罩霧' implies creating an illusion or barrier of mist-like motion to confuse or shield against opponents.

images.original_text:
白撓拖刀勢
乃佯輸詐回鐮法
逆轉硬上騎龍順
步纏攔崩迎封
接進弄鐮就是
中平也破

persons.posture: The figure is in a low, forward-leaning stance with left foot forward and right foot back, knees bent. The body is angled slightly to the left, weight distributed forward. The right arm extends fully backward holding a long staff horizontally, while the left arm is bent and raised near the shoulder, as if preparing for or executing a sweeping motion. The head is turned toward the direction of the staff’s swing, indicating focus on the incoming or outgoing attack.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'White Scratching Dragging Sword Posture' (白撓拖刀勢), a feigned retreat technique designed to lure the opponent into an overcommitment. The practitioner appears to be retreating while dragging the staff behind, then suddenly reverses direction to strike with a powerful upward sweep ('reverse turning hard up, riding dragon posture'). The movement combines evasion with sudden counterattack, using the staff to entangle or block the opponent’s advance, followed by a closing strike that breaks their centerline ('step缠拦崩迎封, 接进弄镰就是中平也破').

images.original_text:
琵琶勢
乃白牛轉角鎗法
上來鉤崩進挫中
來滾剎換孥好下
來提櫓快如梭得
手青龍獻爪

persons.posture: Stance: Low, forward-leaning crouch with left foot forward and right foot back for stability. Body angled slightly forward, weight distributed heavily on front leg. Left hand grips spear near the tip, extending it diagonally upward and forward; right hand holds the shaft closer to the middle. Head turned toward the direction of the spear’s thrust, eyes focused ahead. Arms extended in a controlled, linear motion, suggesting a piercing or hooking technique.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Pipa Posture' (琵琶勢), a spear technique involving a turning angle maneuver ('White Ox Turning Corner Spear Method'). The movement combines a hooking block from above, followed by a downward rolling slash, then a quick transition into a lifting or pulling motion (提櫓) resembling a shuttle's speed. The final phrase 'Hand Green Dragon Presents Claw' suggests a finishing strike or grip technique using the hand after the spear maneuver.

images.original_text:
靈猫捉鼠勢
乃無中生有鎗
法進步虛下撲
纏賺伊鐮動使
梨花過壓桃天
沖打

persons.posture: Crouched stance with left leg forward and right leg back, body low to the ground, weight distributed forward. Right hand grips spear near tip, left hand positioned lower on shaft. Spear extended diagonally forward and downward, pointing toward ground. Head turned slightly left, eyes focused ahead. Appears to be executing a low sweeping or thrusting motion.
persons.tactic: This is the 'Spirit Cat Catching Mouse' posture — a deceptive martial technique using feigned retreat (虚) followed by a sudden forward strike (扑). The spear is used to entangle or disrupt the opponent’s centerline while advancing. The phrase “纏賺伊鐮動使” suggests using circular or winding movements to manipulate the opponent’s weapon or position, while “梨花過壓桃天” evokes a flowing, blossoming motion that overwhelms the target. “沖打” indicates a direct, forceful strike following the maneuver.

images.original_text: 乃鷹捉兇之法勢 雖高發身中變異 任地理伏地蛇沖 我又磨旗掃地

persons.posture: Standing in a low, stable stance with feet shoulder-width apart, body slightly crouched. One arm is raised high above the head, gripping a long spear vertically, tip pointing upward. The other arm is bent at the elbow, hand near the hip. Weight is evenly distributed between both legs, center of gravity lowered for balance and readiness.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the 'Eagle Catching凶' (Eagle Seizing the Fierce) martial technique — a defensive yet aggressive maneuver where the practitioner uses the spear to control space, disrupt opponent’s advance, and prepare for a sweeping or thrusting counterattack. The phrase '我又磨旗掃地' suggests a follow-up motion of sweeping the ground with the spear, possibly to disarm or unbalance an opponent.

images.original_text:
美人認針勢
乃儘頭鎗法好
破蛇防他顛
提起手鳳點頭
披閃認直戳

persons.posture: The figure stands in a forward-facing stance with left foot slightly advanced. The body is angled slightly to the right, weight distributed forward. The right arm extends straight ahead holding a spear pointed forward, while the left arm is bent and held back near the hip for balance. The head is turned slightly toward the spear tip, eyes focused forward. This posture suggests a probing or striking motion with the spear’s tip.
persons.tactic: This illustration depicts the 'Beauty Recognizes Needle Posture' (美人認針勢), a martial technique using the spear to probe and strike. The text advises using the spear's tip to counter an opponent’s snake-like attack (破蛇) by deflecting their advance (防他顛). The movement involves lifting the spear to create an opening (提起手鳳點頭) and then delivering a direct thrust (披閃認直戳) to pierce through defenses.

images.original_text:
蒼龍擺尾勢
乃棚退救護
之法電轉風
回鷺散梨花
閃賺

persons.posture: Stance: Low, wide-legged horse stance with body angled slightly forward. Left leg bent, right leg extended back for balance. Arms extended: left hand gripping staff near the middle, right hand at the tip of the staff. Staff held diagonally across body from upper right to lower left. Head turned toward the direction of the staff’s tip, eyes focused ahead. Weight distributed mostly on the front (left) leg, rear (right) leg providing support and stability.
persons.tactic: This is a defensive maneuver known as 'Cang Long Bai Wei' (Azure Dragon Swings Tail), used to evade an opponent’s attack while simultaneously preparing a counter-strike. The motion involves pivoting the body and rotating the staff in a circular arc to deflect or redirect incoming force — described here as 'electric wind turning' — followed by a rapid retreat ('棚退') and a sweeping strike ('回鷺散梨花'), which mimics the scattering petals of plum blossoms, indicating a fast, flowing, and unpredictable movement pattern designed to confuse and disorient the opponent.

images.original_text:
伏虎勢
乃六封鎗法
斜倒硬上如
風退閃提攔
纏捉他如壓
師又朝天鐵
掃迎封接靠

persons.posture: The figure is in a low, forward-leaning stance with left leg bent and right leg extended back for balance. The body is angled forward, weight shifted onto the front foot. The right arm extends forward holding a spear pointed downward at an angle, while the left arm is drawn back for counterbalance. The head is turned slightly toward the spear tip, eyes focused ahead.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the 'Fuhu Posture' (伏虎勢), a defensive and offensive spear technique. The movement involves using the spear to intercept or deflect an opponent’s attack (‘風退閃提攔’ — wind retreat, flash sidestep, lift and block) while simultaneously applying pressure to control or trap the opponent’s weapon or limb ('纏捉他如壓' — entangle and press down). The final motion described is a sweeping upward strike or sweep to clear or repel ('掃迎封接靠' — sweep to meet, block, receive, and lean against), suggesting a transition from defense to offense.

images.original_text:
推山塞海勢
乃護膝鎗法高來
搖旗換棍低來鉞
簫顛提中來如箭
有虛真可用鉄牛
耕地

persons.posture: Stance: Wide horse stance with knees bent, body leaning forward slightly. Left hand grips the spear shaft near the middle, right hand positioned lower on the shaft. Spear held diagonally downward to the left, tip pointing toward ground. Weight distributed evenly between both feet, core engaged for stability and power transfer.
persons.tactic: This is a martial application of the 'Push Mountain, Block Sea' spear technique, emphasizing dynamic movement—rising (high spear) and lowering (low spear) to disrupt opponent’s balance. The text suggests using the spear in conjunction with flag signals (symbolic or tactical cues), and the phrase 'like an arrow' implies rapid thrusting motion. The final line about 'iron ox' and 'plowing land' may metaphorically refer to overwhelming force or agricultural discipline applied in combat.

images.original_text:
鴞子撲鴿鷺勢
乃撥草尋蛇鎗法
高接雖用經擊逢
中披擦直過倘他
棚退把鎗還滾手
中平一剎

persons.posture: Kneeling in a low, crouched stance with one knee on the ground and the other leg extended backward for balance. The body is angled forward, weight distributed low to maintain stability. The right hand grips the spear shaft near the middle, extending it horizontally forward, while the left hand supports or guides the spear near the tip. The head is turned slightly toward the direction of the spear’s point, indicating focus on the target.
persons.tactic: This posture represents a defensive yet aggressive maneuver known as 'Pigeon-Flapping-Dove-Strike' (鴞子撲鴿鷺勢), where the practitioner uses a spear to intercept an opponent's attack by deflecting or parrying with a high block ('高接'), then transitions into a sweeping or piercing motion ('經擊逢') to strike through the opponent’s guard. The technique emphasizes fluidity — from blocking to advancing — using a low center of gravity to absorb force and deliver a decisive thrust ('中平一剎').

images.original_text:
太公釣魚勢
乃磨旗鎗法諸勢可敵輕挨緩捉順敵提挐進退如風剛柔得體

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance with left foot forward, body slightly crouched. Right hand grips the spear shaft near the middle, left hand holds the tip of the spear. Spear is extended diagonally forward and downward. Weight distributed evenly between both feet, torso upright with slight lean forward to maintain balance and readiness for offensive or defensive movement.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Tai Gong Fishing' martial posture, a technique for engaging an opponent by using the spear to control distance and apply pressure. The description suggests combining defensive retreat ('退') with aggressive advance ('進'), maintaining fluidity like wind and flexibility like softness, indicating a dynamic interplay of yielding and striking.

images.original_text: 手之上下服拆往待高長鏡將及身攜標束之中與不中敵必用鎗顛撥我即乘隙徑進急取出刀在右隨解砍殺一入鎗身之內則鎗為棄物我必勝彼矣但擲標後而倉皇不及取刀是一大病其禦短兵更易

右牌用藤云 木牌皮牌皆類此用
右標鎗或用樁木細竹皆可但前重而後輕前稍粗而後稍細為得法
開札衣勢 此起手勢也 照高管下橫 行直進諸勢 可變有躲閃之妙

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance with left foot slightly advanced, body angled slightly to the right. Right arm raised high holding a curved sword above the head, left arm extended outward holding a shield in front of the torso. Weight distributed evenly between both feet, knees slightly bent for stability and readiness.
persons.tactic: This depicts a defensive-offensive posture known as 'Open Zha Yi Posture' (開札衣勢), where the practitioner raises the sword overhead while using the shield to block or deflect incoming attacks. The text suggests that from this position, one may advance straight or shift sideways to evade or counterattack — hence the phrase 'can change to dodge and strike' (可變有躲閃之妙).

images.original_text:
斜行勢
此刀直來橫
受之法動偏
步上硬騎龍
以進人之左

persons.posture: The figure stands in a low, forward-leaning stance with left foot forward and right foot back, knees bent. The body is angled slightly to the left, weight distributed forward. The right arm extends upward holding a curved sword pointed diagonally upward, while the left arm holds a shield raised high on the left side, blocking or deflecting an incoming attack. The head is turned toward the direction of the sword’s motion, indicating focus on the opponent’s advance.
persons.tactic: This depicts a defensive counterattack technique where the practitioner uses a shield to block an incoming straight strike (likely from an opponent’s sword) and simultaneously counters with a diagonal upward slash using the sword. The movement is described as 'stepping forward while hard-riding the dragon' — suggesting a dynamic, powerful advance combined with precise timing to evade and strike.

images.original_text:
此仙人指路勢
乃看管之法如
步直進直退諸
勢可變

persons.posture: The figure stands in a forward-leaning stance with left foot forward and right foot back, knees bent. The body leans slightly forward with weight distributed mostly on the front leg. The right arm holds a sword raised diagonally upward, blade pointing toward upper right. The left arm holds a shield extended outward to the left side, angled to block or deflect. Head is turned slightly toward the direction of the sword’s thrust, eyes focused ahead.
persons.tactic: This posture represents a defensive-offensive martial technique known as 'Xianren Zhilu' (Immortal's Finger Path), where the practitioner uses the shield to parry incoming attacks while preparing to counter with the sword. The movement allows for both defense and transition into offensive strikes, with the ability to shift forward or retreat depending on the opponent’s response.

images.original_text:
滾解法
此勢隨滾進以
襲人之右先進
刀後進腳疾速
如風為妙

persons.posture: The figure is in a low, forward-leaning stance with the left foot advanced and the right foot trailing. The body is angled slightly to the left, weight distributed forward onto the front leg. The right arm extends forward holding a curved sword raised above shoulder height, blade pointing diagonally upward. The left arm is bent and held close to the torso for balance. The head is turned toward the direction of attack, eyes focused ahead.
persons.tactic: This depicts a rolling or advancing strike technique ('滾解法') where the practitioner moves forward while simultaneously striking with the sword. The text instructs that the attacker should advance with the right foot first, followed by the sword, then the left foot, mimicking the speed and fluidity of wind — indicating a rapid, continuous offensive maneuver designed to overwhelm the opponent’s defense.

images.original_text:
躍步勢
此乃騎龍如
振馬刀前腳
後誘人來轉
過牌來刀在
後低平坐下
靠和挨

persons.posture: The figure is in a dynamic forward leap, left leg extended forward with knee bent, right leg trailing behind. The body leans slightly forward with weight shifted onto the front foot. The left arm extends forward holding a sword pointed upward, while the right arm holds a shield raised to the side. Head turned slightly toward the direction of movement, eyes focused ahead.
persons.tactic: This posture represents a 'leaping step' technique (躍步勢) designed to simulate riding a dragon — a metaphor for swift, agile movement. The practitioner uses the sword to strike or threaten from an elevated angle while simultaneously using the shield to deflect or block incoming attacks. The maneuver is followed by a feint (誘人來轉) to draw the opponent into a vulnerable position, then a low sweeping cut (過牌來刀) as the attacker drops to a lower stance (後低平坐下), preparing for a close-range grappling or finishing move (靠和挨).

images.original_text:
低平勢
此真正對敵勢
也用推步須要
帶標一根身在
腳內標步齊進
百發百中

persons.posture: Kneeling or low crouching stance with left knee on ground, right leg bent and foot planted. Body angled forward, weight distributed low and centered. Left arm extended holding a shield (shaped like a kite or fan), right arm thrusting forward holding a long staff horizontally. Head turned slightly toward the direction of the staff’s tip, eyes focused ahead.
persons.tactic: This is a low defensive-offensive posture used to counter an opponent’s advance. The shield provides protection while the staff delivers a thrusting strike. The movement emphasizes stepping forward with the inner foot in coordination with the staff thrust, ensuring balance and precision — hence the phrase “百發百中” (hundred shots, hundred hits).

images.original_text:
金雞畔頭勢
畔頭之勢最為良
銜上頭從牌下藏進步如風
人莫變刀銓牌
開為難當

persons.posture: The figure is in a low, forward-leaning stance with left leg bent and right leg extended back for balance. The body is angled sideways, weight shifted forward onto the front foot. The left arm holds a large, curved shield horizontally in front of the upper body, covering the head and torso. The right hand grips a short weapon (possibly a knife or dagger) at the hip, ready to strike or defend. The head is turned slightly toward the shield, indicating alertness and readiness to counterattack or evade.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the 'Golden Chicken Head Position' (金雞畔頭勢), a defensive maneuver using the shield to block incoming attacks while advancing with a swift step. The text advises that this stance is highly effective ('最為良'), as the attacker’s head is targeted by the shield's edge ('銜上頭'), and the defender advances like wind ('下藏進步如風'). The opponent must be wary ('人莫變刀銓牌') because the shield blocks all openings ('開為難當').

images.original_text:
埋伏勢
此勢進步甚速
用小行或左或
右如有饒戯在
解不能脫手急
用刀尖將解借
力頂開急進絕
如

persons.posture: Kneeling in a low crouch, body angled forward with weight shifted to the front leg. Left hand grips a large curved shield held horizontally in front of the torso. Right hand holds a long sword pointed diagonally upward and backward, blade edge facing outward. Head turned slightly toward the direction of advance, eyes focused ahead. This posture suggests a stealthy, aggressive ambush stance ready to strike or defend.
persons.tactic: Ambush technique — advancing swiftly while using shield for defense and sword for sudden counterattack. The text advises rapid movement and using the sword tip to break free if entangled, then pressing forward with force to cut off retreat.

images.original_text:
中平勢
此勢前弓後箭
陰陽要轉兩手
要直推步如風
天下莫敵

persons.posture: Stance: Front bow, back arrow — left leg forward in a low horse stance, right leg extended back. Body angled slightly forward with torso leaning into the movement. Arms extended straight ahead, gripping a spear horizontally with both hands. Weight distributed heavily on front leg, rear leg grounded for stability. Feet planted firmly, toes pointed forward, knees bent to maintain balance and power.
persons.tactic: This is the 'Middle Level Posture' (中平勢), a foundational martial stance emphasizing balance and readiness. The practitioner uses the spear to push forward with full body weight, simulating a powerful thrust or strike. The phrase '阴阳要转两手' suggests rotational energy transfer through the arms and torso, while '要直推步如风' indicates rapid, linear advancement with the spear. The final line '天下莫敵' declares this technique as invincible.

images.original_text:
騎龍勢
閉門之法上騎龍
下閘高築大有功
誤若當前拔一下
勸君眼快腳如風

persons.posture: The figure stands in a forward-facing, low stance with legs bent and weight distributed forward. The right arm extends straight ahead holding a spear horizontally, while the left arm is drawn back for balance. The body leans slightly forward, head turned to face the direction of the spear’s tip. Feet are planted wide apart, toes pointing forward, indicating readiness for thrust or defensive maneuver.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Riding Dragon' martial stance, a defensive technique involving closing the door (blocking an opponent’s advance) by raising the spear high above the head (‘high筑’), then lowering it swiftly to intercept or strike. The text advises quick eye movement and footwork to respond to an opponent’s misstep — suggesting a reactive counterattack after observing the enemy’s error.

images.original_text:
鉤開勢
鉤法由來阻大門
小門挫下向前奔
若還他使低來勢
閒挫憑君利便分

persons.posture: Stance: Wide, grounded stance with left foot forward and right foot back, knees bent for stability. Body angled slightly forward, weight distributed low to maintain balance. Right arm extended forward holding spear shaft; left arm bent at elbow, hand near hip for support. Spear held horizontally, tip pointing left, ready to strike or control opponent’s movement.
persons.tactic: This is the 'Hook Open' technique (鉤開勢), where the practitioner uses a spear to block or redirect an incoming attack by hooking it downward and then advancing forward. The text instructs that if the opponent lowers their posture, the defender should use this opportunity to create space or gain advantage through controlled displacement.

images.original_text:
架上勢
鎗打高來須用架
架特管上又管下
陰陽反覆腳如風
鉄柱金剛也戯怕

persons.posture: Crouched stance with left leg forward and right leg back, body low and angled slightly forward. Both hands grip a long spear horizontally, extending it forward with the tip pointing left. The head is turned slightly toward the spear’s direction, eyes focused ahead. Weight is distributed heavily on the front leg, creating a stable, grounded posture for defense or counterattack.
persons.tactic: This depicts the 'Jia Shang Shi' (Frame Above Posture), a defensive technique using a spear to intercept an opponent’s high attack by raising the weapon to block, then transitioning into a downward strike or controlling motion. The text emphasizes using the spear to manage both high and low attacks while maintaining dynamic footwork ('yin-yang reversal') and stability ('iron pillar, golden warrior'), suggesting a combination of blocking, redirecting, and striking.

images.original_text:
閘下勢
閘勢緣何要挈腳
挈腳乃是起步法
連身坐下向前沖
上面不着下面着

persons.posture: The figure is in a low, forward-leaning stance with one leg bent and the other extended back. The body is lowered as if sitting down, facing forward. The right hand grips the spear near the tip, extending it horizontally to the left, while the left hand supports the shaft closer to the center. Weight is distributed heavily on the front leg, with the rear leg providing balance and propulsion. The head is turned slightly toward the direction of movement, eyes focused ahead.
persons.tactic: This posture demonstrates a foundational martial technique called '閘下勢' (Gate-Down Stance), used for initiating forward冲 (charge) after a preparatory step. The '挈腳' (lifting the foot) refers to lifting the rear foot to initiate motion, followed by a coordinated lowering of the body into a seated-like position while driving forward with the spear. The instruction '上面不着下面着' implies that the spear should be kept low and close to the ground to avoid being intercepted or to strike low targets.

images.original_text:
拗步退勢
直進直出居須記
站住即是中平勢
高低左右任居行
切挫鉤閘毋輕易

persons.posture: The figure is in a forward stepping stance, left leg extended forward with knee bent, right leg trailing behind. Body is slightly crouched, weight shifted forward onto the front leg. The right arm extends forward holding a spear horizontally, while the left arm is drawn back for balance. Head is turned slightly toward the direction of movement, eyes focused ahead. The spear is held with both hands near the shaft’s midpoint, ready for thrust or defensive maneuver.
persons.tactic: This posture illustrates the 'Nì Bù Tuì Shì' (拗步退勢) — a retreat step technique that maintains central balance ('Zhōng Píng Shì') while moving forward or sideways. The text advises against hasty or careless use of techniques like cutting, pressing, hooking, or blocking, emphasizing controlled application during movement.

images.original_text:
過人最打亦可
一雙人大門對打他力雄我急變丁字步打用身壓之然後變
一他小門殺來急我坐進前腳就他棍中滾入連
剪二三下然後殺
一把大門空起勾下勾步絕妙又有下流水勾不又他
陰手
陰手
陰手
陽手
手劍
手劍
手劍

按高擲一脈磕——拔後手一尺剝一隻殺接低打來亦然
一直破對打脈磕帶抽後手剝相連後進殺
一入中攔只用一脈磕帶晨拔剝五寸一進殺若未侵入他棍未死亦用拔用手一尺剝下進殺
一踏過他小門進入如前法但自棍橫勢送進上中攔皆然
當上起
此當字如曲中之拍位妙不可言故贊之曰我既他傍前手直當後直加拔有神在中學到此一貫乎萬矣千千萬萬步俱有拍位一轉陰陽不可太早臨時一下乃不費力明之明之折腳不如直入右李良欽之傳學到此一貫乎萬矣左劉師協之傳中間有拍位不用拔劍先落只拍位起

拍手殺則又緊矣但無因死人棍之法大抵前用拔剝為是小門亦然
左右來俱有拍位
右在偏頭關時得之教師林琰者其詩曰壯士執金鎗只用九寸長日日打一轉好將見閻王
三教師原來合一家
一千言萬語不外乎致人而不致於人一句李良
扁身中攔勢

persons.posture: Standing in a forward-facing stance with left foot forward, body slightly crouched. Right arm extended forward holding a staff horizontally at chest level, left arm bent back near hip. Weight distributed mostly on front leg, torso angled slightly forward to engage opponent.
persons.tactic: Executing a mid-level block or interception technique using the staff to deflect an incoming strike from the opponent’s weapon.

persons.posture: Facing left, standing in a parallel stance with feet shoulder-width apart. Both arms extended forward, holding a staff horizontally across the body at waist height. Body upright, eyes focused on opponent, ready to push or parry.
persons.tactic: Engaging in a direct confrontation, applying pressure with the staff to maintain control of the centerline and counter the opponent’s offensive movement.

images.original_text: 大當勢

persons.posture: Standing in a low, wide stance with knees bent and body slightly crouched. Left foot forward, right foot back, weight distributed evenly. Both arms extended forward holding a staff, crossed over opponent’s staff. Head turned to the right, eyes focused on opponent. Staff held horizontally at chest height, ready to parry or strike.
persons.tactic: Executing a defensive 'Great Block' technique (大當勢), using the staff to intercept and deflect an incoming attack while maintaining balance and readiness to counterattack.

persons.posture: In a similar low, wide stance with left foot forward and right foot back. Body angled slightly forward, weight centered. Arms extended holding a staff, crossing opponent’s staff diagonally. Head turned left, eyes locked on opponent. Staff positioned to apply pressure or redirect force.
persons.tactic: Engaging in a simultaneous offensive and defensive maneuver, using the staff to control distance and disrupt opponent’s rhythm, preparing for a follow-up strike or sweep.

images.original_text: 大頓勢

persons.posture: Standing in a low, wide stance with knees bent and body slightly crouched. Weight is distributed forward on the front leg, rear leg grounded for stability. The left arm extends forward holding a staff horizontally, while the right arm pulls back to brace or prepare for counterattack. Head turned slightly toward opponent, eyes focused on the adversary.
persons.tactic: This is a defensive or preparatory stance in a staff combat sequence, likely designed to intercept or parry an incoming strike while maintaining balance and readiness to advance or retreat. The posture suggests control of distance and timing.

persons.posture: In a similar low, wide stance with knees bent and body angled slightly forward. Both arms extend forward, gripping a staff that crosses diagonally over the opponent’s weapon. The front foot is planted firmly, rear foot pivoted for rotational power. Body leans into the engagement, suggesting an offensive thrust or binding maneuver.
persons.tactic: This figure appears to be executing a binding or trapping technique with the staff, aiming to control the opponent’s weapon and create an opening for a strike or takedown. The crossed-staff position indicates a classic martial engagement meant to neutralize the opponent’s attack.

images.original_text: 僊人捧盤勢

persons.posture: Standing in a low, wide stance with feet shoulder-width apart, knees bent. Body angled slightly forward, weight distributed evenly. Right arm extended forward holding a staff horizontally at chest level; left arm bent and drawn back near hip. Head turned to face opponent, eyes focused on the staff tip.
persons.tactic: Executing a defensive or intercepting technique with the staff, preparing to parry or counterattack by controlling the centerline of engagement.

persons.posture: In a strong, grounded stance with legs spread wide, knees deeply bent. Staff held high above head with both hands, arms fully extended upward, body leaning slightly backward for leverage. Feet firmly planted, weight shifted toward rear leg to generate power from the hips and core.
persons.tactic: Performing an overhead striking or pressing maneuver, aiming to dominate the opponent’s weapon line and force them into a defensive position.

images.original_text: 大吊勢

persons.posture: Wide horse stance with knees bent, body leaning forward, left arm extended holding staff horizontally, right arm pulled back in guard position; weight distributed evenly between both feet, head turned slightly toward opponent.
persons.tactic: Executing a pulling or trapping technique using the staff to control opponent’s weapon, preparing for a counter-strike or throw.

persons.posture: Similar wide horse stance, body angled slightly backward, left arm extending staff forward to engage opponent’s staff, right arm braced near hip for balance; weight centered with slight emphasis on rear leg for stability and leverage.
persons.tactic: Engaging in a direct staff-to-staff confrontation, attempting to overpower or redirect opponent’s force through controlled pressure and footwork.

images.original_text: 齊眉殺勢

persons.posture: Wide horse stance with left leg forward, body slightly crouched and turned sideways. Right arm extended forward holding a staff horizontally at chest level; left arm bent back near hip for balance. Weight distributed mostly on front leg, eyes focused forward toward opponent.
persons.tactic: Executing a low-level staff strike or block aimed at the opponent’s legs or midsection, preparing for follow-up thrust or sweep.

persons.posture: Wide horse stance with right leg forward, body angled slightly backward for stability. Both arms extended forward holding a staff horizontally at shoulder height, staff aligned parallel to ground. Weight balanced evenly between both legs, head upright and facing opponent.
persons.tactic: Maintaining a defensive or counter-attacking posture with staff held in ready position, possibly preparing to parry or initiate a thrusting attack.

images.original_text: 滴水勢

persons.posture: Stance: Wide horse stance with knees bent, body slightly crouched and leaning forward. Right hand holds staff horizontally in front, left hand grips staff near the end, extending it backward. Weight distributed evenly between both feet, facing opponent diagonally to the right. Staff held low and ready for parry or strike.
persons.tactic: Defensive counter-attack using staff to intercept incoming strike while maintaining balance and readiness to advance.

persons.posture: Stance: Forward lunging step with right leg extended, left knee bent deeply. Body leans forward aggressively, staff raised high above head with both hands, poised to strike downward. Eyes focused on opponent’s upper body. Weight shifted heavily onto front leg for maximum force generation.
persons.tactic: Offensive overhead strike technique, aiming to break guard or knock opponent off balance with a powerful descending blow.

images.original_text: 直符送書勢

persons.posture: Wide horse stance with knees bent, body slightly crouched and leaning forward. Left hand grips staff near the end, right hand extends staff forward. Weight distributed evenly between both feet, facing opponent directly.
persons.tactic: Executing a forward thrusting technique with staff, aiming to control distance and initiate offensive pressure.

persons.posture: Similar wide horse stance, body angled slightly backward for balance. Both hands grip staff near the middle, extending it horizontally toward opponent. Eyes focused on adversary, ready to parry or counterattack.
persons.tactic: Defensive posture with staff extended to intercept incoming strike, preparing for simultaneous counter-thrust or displacement maneuver.

images.original_text: 走馬回頭勢

persons.posture: Wide horse stance, body turned slightly left, weight distributed evenly on both legs. Right hand grips staff near the middle, left hand extends forward to hold the staff’s tip. Staff held horizontally at waist height, angled slightly downward toward opponent. Head turned right, eyes focused on opponent.
persons.tactic: Executing a 'Horse-riding回头势' (Zou Ma Hui Tou Shi) — a defensive counterattack technique where the practitioner uses a staff to intercept and redirect an incoming strike while pivoting their body to reposition for a反击 (counter-strike).

persons.posture: Slightly crouched forward stance, knees bent, weight shifted onto front leg. Both hands grip staff near the center, staff held horizontally across chest level, pointing leftward. Body leans forward with shoulders squared, head facing left, preparing to thrust or sweep.
persons.tactic: Engaging in a direct offensive maneuver using the staff to apply pressure or initiate a sweeping attack. The posture suggests readiness to advance or unbalance the opponent through controlled force.

images.original_text: 一棍對一棍，兩相交鋒。左者前弓步，右足蹬地，右手持棍上挑，左手助勢；右者後馬步，左足前踏，左手持棍下格，右手助勢。雙棍交叉於中，力爭上下之機。

persons.posture: Front bow stance with right foot forward, left foot back; body slightly crouched, weight distributed forward. Right hand holds staff high in an upward thrusting motion, left hand extended backward for balance and force generation.
persons.tactic: Executing a high-level offensive strike while maintaining stability and readiness to counterattack.

persons.posture: Back horse stance with left foot forward, right foot rear; body low and stable, weight centered. Left hand holds staff low in a defensive block or parry, right hand supporting the grip for control and power transfer.
persons.tactic: Defending against the opponent’s high attack while preparing to counter with a low strike or sweep.

images.original_text: 倒頭勢

persons.posture: Standing in a low, wide stance with feet shoulder-width apart, knees bent. Body angled slightly forward, weight distributed evenly. Right arm extended forward holding a staff horizontally, left arm pulled back for balance. Staff held near the center, pointing toward opponent.
persons.tactic: Executing a defensive or counter-attacking posture in close-quarters staff combat, preparing to parry or strike with the staff while maintaining stability and readiness to advance or retreat.

persons.posture: In a crouched, aggressive stance with one leg forward and the other back, body leaning forward with weight on the front foot. Both arms engaged: right hand grips staff near the end, extending it diagonally upward; left hand supports or braces the staff near the middle. Head turned toward opponent, eyes focused on engagement.
persons.tactic: Initiating an offensive thrust or sweeping strike with the staff, aiming to disrupt opponent’s balance or create an opening for follow-up techniques. The posture suggests a dynamic, momentum-driven attack.

images.original_text: 下成勢勢劍

persons.posture: Standing in a low, wide stance with left foot forward and right foot back, body angled slightly to the left. Right arm extended forward holding a staff horizontally, left arm bent at the elbow with hand near hip. Weight distributed mostly on the front leg, torso leaning slightly forward for balance and attack.
persons.tactic: Executing a horizontal staff strike aimed at the opponent’s midsection or head, while maintaining defensive posture with the rear hand ready to adjust or parry.

persons.posture: In a low crouch with knees bent, feet spread apart for stability. Both arms extended forward holding a staff in a defensive or counter-attacking position, staff angled upward to intercept or deflect the incoming strike. Body leans slightly backward to absorb force and prepare for a return movement.
persons.tactic: Defensive parry or interception using the staff to block the opponent’s attack, preparing for a counter-strike or footwork adjustment to reposition.

images.original_text: 閃腰剪勢

persons.posture: Standing in a low, wide horse stance with knees bent and body slightly crouched. Left foot forward, right foot back. Both arms extended forward holding a staff horizontally, crossed with opponent’s staff. Head turned left, eyes focused on opponent. Weight distributed evenly between both legs, ready to pivot or advance.
persons.tactic: This is the 'Flash Waist Cut Posture' (閃腰剪勢), a close-combat technique where the practitioner uses a staff to intercept and control the opponent’s weapon by crossing it diagonally, then pivoting to strike or unbalance the opponent using hip rotation.

persons.posture: In a similar low horse stance, facing left. Right foot forward, left foot back. Arms extended forward holding a staff, crossed with opponent’s staff. Body angled slightly backward for balance, head turned right, eyes locked on opponent. Weight centered over front leg, preparing for counter-movement or force redirection.
persons.tactic: The opposing practitioner mirrors the first, engaging in a staff cross to control distance and initiate a rotational strike or sweep. The posture emphasizes leverage through hip movement ('flash waist') to generate power from the core rather than arm strength alone.

images.original_text:
對敵圖
左為持槍者，右為持槍者。二人相向而立，槍尖相交，步法穩健，重心下沉，攻防兼備。

persons.posture: Left-facing stance with feet shoulder-width apart, left foot forward, right foot back. Body slightly crouched, weight distributed evenly. Right arm extended forward holding spear tip pointed diagonally upward; left arm bent at elbow, hand near hip for balance. Head turned toward opponent, eyes focused on target.
persons.tactic: Simultaneous offensive and defensive spear technique — advancing with controlled pressure while maintaining guard posture to counter incoming strikes.

persons.posture: Right-facing stance, feet parallel, slightly wider than shoulder-width. Slight squat with knees bent, center of gravity low. Right arm thrusts spear forward in direct line with opponent’s weapon; left arm drawn back for stability. Torso angled slightly inward, head alert and facing opponent.
persons.tactic: Counter-attack using spear thrust from a stable base — aiming to pierce or deflect the opponent’s attack while preserving balance and readiness for follow-up motion.

images.original_text:
實握射圖
此法弓滿左
肱直如弦而
弓斜如月前
平奶頭

persons.posture: Standing in a stable, grounded stance with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. The body is angled slightly forward, weight distributed evenly. The left arm extends straight back holding the bowstring, while the right arm draws the bowstring to the cheek. The bow is held horizontally, fully drawn, with the bow limb curved outward. Head turned slightly toward the target, eyes focused ahead.
persons.tactic: This depicts the proper form for drawing and aiming a bow — known as '实握射' (solid grip shooting). The posture emphasizes stability, alignment of the arm and torso, and precise control of the draw to ensure accuracy.

persons.posture: Similar stance to Person Left: feet planted firmly, knees bent, body aligned for balance. The right arm pulls the bowstring back to the ear, left arm holds the bow steady. The bow is drawn to full extension, limbs arched like a crescent moon. The head is turned slightly toward the direction of aim, maintaining focus on the target.
persons.tactic: This illustrates the same archery technique from a different angle or as a mirror image, reinforcing correct form. The description '弓斜如月前' suggests the bow should resemble a crescent moon when drawn — indicating optimal tension and form for maximum power and precision.
